evals.prompt: Write a LinkedIn article for our VP of Engineering persona at mid-market B2B SaaS companies (200-1000 employees). Company context: Arcline Analytics is a data observability platform that detects pipeline breakages and schema drift before they hit dashboards. Topic: Why most data teams discover broken dashboards from a Slack message, not from their monitoring stack, and what the fix looks like. Goal: thought leadership / awareness. Brand voice: direct, opinionated, no hedging. Include a specific CTA to follow our company page for the weekly 'Data Ops in Practice' series. Do not fabricate any statistics -- use placeholders if needed.
evals.expected_output: A LinkedIn article between 600-1200 words with a scroll-stopping first line (question, stat, or counterintuitive claim), short paragraphs of 2-4 sentences, bold key lines for scanning, a clear personal POV that takes a position rather than hedging, and a closing question to drive comments. Must include a CTA to follow the Arcline Analytics company page for the 'Data Ops in Practice' series. No banned words. No fabricated statistics.
expectations: Content performance: The opening line is a specific, scroll-stopping hook (a concrete stat, a named pain scenario like 'Your CFO found the data bug before your monitoring did', or a counterintuitive claim) -- not a generic opener like 'Data quality is important' or 'In today's world' that would get scrolled past in a LinkedIn feed

Brand consistency: The article maintains a direct, opinionated brand voice throughout -- takes a clear position on what the fix looks like with specific recommendations, never hedges with 'it depends' or 'on the other hand', and contains zero banned words (leverage, synergy, best-in-class, seamless, robust, end-to-end, game-changing, revolutionary, disruptive, holistic, cutting-edge, world-class, innovative, empower, unlock, transform, solution, ecosystem, paradigm, circle back, reach out)

Content performance: The article is written specifically for VP of Engineering at mid-market B2B SaaS personas -- references their specific world (monitoring stacks, dbt models, pipeline breakages, team size dynamics of 200-1000 employee companies) rather than generic data quality advice that could target anyone

Pipeline contribution: The CTA to follow Arcline Analytics for the 'Data Ops in Practice' series is woven naturally into the article's narrative (not bolted on as an afterthought) and gives a specific reason to follow -- this converts a one-time reader into a recurring audience member in the awareness funnel

Content performance: The article ends with a specific, debatable question designed to drive comments (e.g., 'What's the longest your dashboards were stale before someone noticed?' not 'What do you think?') -- comment-driving closers are the primary organic reach multiplier on LinkedIn

Production efficiency: The article is between 600-1200 words, uses short paragraphs (2-4 sentences max) with bold key lines for scanning, and is ready to publish on LinkedIn without structural editing -- a marketing team member could copy-paste this directly, not treat it as a rough draft

evals.prompt: Write a case study for Arcline Analytics. Customer: Meridian Logistics, a $340M revenue freight brokerage with 1,200 employees and a 14-person data engineering team. Challenge: Their BI dashboards showed stale revenue numbers for an average of 6 hours per incident because schema changes in upstream ERP migrations broke 23 dbt models silently. They discovered issues only when the CFO's Monday board report showed obviously wrong numbers. Why they chose Arcline: Evaluated Arcline against Monte Carlo and Bigeye; chose Arcline because it was the only tool that could monitor schema changes at the source connector level before data entered the warehouse. Implementation: Deployed Arcline agents on 4 Fivetran connectors and 38 dbt models over a 3-week rollout. Results: Mean time to detect pipeline breakage dropped from 6 hours to 8 minutes. False positive rate held at 4%. Zero stale-dashboard incidents in the 90 days post-deployment. CFO quote: 'I used to start every Monday checking whether the numbers were real. Now I just read them.' Write this for a Director of Data Engineering persona at companies doing ERP migrations. Goal: sales enablement.
evals.expected_output: A case study between 600-1200 words following the exact structure: outcome-first headline, Challenge section with quantified specifics, Why They Chose You section with honest competitive context, Implementation section, Results section with the three quantified metrics provided, an authentic customer quote, and Next Steps section. Contains a clear CTA. No banned words. No fabricated data beyond what was provided.
expectations: Pipeline contribution: The headline leads with the specific quantified outcome ('How Meridian Logistics Cut Pipeline Detection Time from 6 Hours to 8 Minutes' pattern) -- not a generic title like 'Meridian Logistics Case Study' or 'How Arcline Helped Meridian' -- because outcome-first headlines are what get a sales rep's email opened when they send this to a prospect

Campaign ROI: The 'Why They Chose You' section names Monte Carlo and Bigeye by name and states the specific technical differentiator (source-connector-level schema monitoring before data enters the warehouse) -- this is the section prospects actually read to decide if a sales call is worth taking, and vague competitive positioning ('we were the best fit') provides zero decision-support value

Pipeline contribution: The case study is written for Directors of Data Engineering at companies doing ERP migrations specifically -- the Challenge section frames the problem in ERP migration language (upstream schema changes, dbt model breakage, connector-level monitoring) so a prospect in that exact situation sees themselves in the story

Campaign ROI: The Results section presents all three quantified metrics (6 hours to 8 minutes detection time, 4% false positive rate, zero stale-dashboard incidents in 90 days) with before/after framing that a sales rep can quote in a discovery call or a prospect can use to build an internal business case

Production efficiency: The case study follows the complete mandated structure in order (outcome-first headline, Challenge, Why They Chose You, Implementation, Results, Quote, Next Steps) and is between 600-1200 words -- a sales enablement team can upload this directly to their content library without restructuring

Content performance: The CFO quote is placed for maximum impact (not buried in the middle of the Implementation section) and the Next Steps section includes a specific CTA targeted at Directors of Data Engineering doing ERP migrations -- not a generic 'contact us' that gives no reason to act now

evals.prompt: We just published a whitepaper titled 'The Hidden Cost of Schema Drift: How Unmonitored Data Pipeline Changes Erode Trust in BI' for Arcline Analytics. The whitepaper covers: (1) quantified cost of stale dashboards -- average enterprise loses 11.4 hours/week of analyst time investigating data freshness issues (source: Arcline internal survey of 200 data teams, Q3 2025), (2) the three failure modes of schema drift: silent column drops, type coercion errors, and upstream rename propagation, (3) why monitoring at the warehouse layer is too late -- you need source-connector-level observability, (4) a framework called 'Shift-Left Data Quality' for catching breakages at ingestion, (5) two customer examples: Meridian Logistics (freight, $340M rev) cut detection time from 6 hours to 8 minutes; Strata Financial (fintech, $89M rev) eliminated 100% of their monthly compliance report corrections. Target persona: VP Data / Director Data Engineering at mid-market companies. Produce the full content multiplication asset tree from this whitepaper.
evals.expected_output: A complete content multiplication asset tree producing exactly 10 derivative assets from the whitepaper: 1 LinkedIn long-form article (900 words, key findings), 3 CEO/VP personal LinkedIn posts (400 words each, different angles), 1 email newsletter (summary with link to full paper), 1 social carousel outline (8 slides, one finding per slide), 1 cold email hook (50 words, one stat as conversation opener), 1 sales one-pager, 5 ad copy variants (LinkedIn, different hooks), 8 email subject line variants (for A/B testing), 1 webinar outline (built from paper's framework), 1 FAQ post. Each asset must include a CTA, use no banned words, and not fabricate statistics beyond those provided.
expectations: Production efficiency: All 10 derivative asset types are produced from a single whitepaper brief (LinkedIn long-form article, 3 CEO/VP LinkedIn posts, email newsletter, social carousel outline, cold email hook, sales one-pager, 5 ad copy variants, 8 email subject line variants, webinar outline, FAQ post) -- this is the core time-saving value of content multiplication; missing any asset type means the marketing team still has to create it manually

Content performance: The 3 CEO/VP LinkedIn posts each take a genuinely different angle on the whitepaper content (e.g., one on the 11.4 hours/week cost quantification, one on the Shift-Left Data Quality framework, one on customer proof points) with different hooks -- posting the same message three times with different words wastes executive publishing slots and trains the algorithm that your content is repetitive

Campaign ROI: The 5 ad copy variants test meaningfully different hooks (e.g., pain-point stat, framework name, customer result, counterintuitive claim, question) with each variant implicitly or explicitly testable as an A/B pair -- without distinct angles, the marketing team is spending ad budget on creative rotation that generates no learning about what resonates with the ICP

Campaign ROI: The 8 email subject line variants comply with the 50-character mobile display limit and span at least 3 of the 5 copywriting frameworks (Specificity, Curiosity, Relevance, Urgency, Question) -- subject lines that exceed mobile character limits get truncated and lose their hook, and testing within a single framework produces marginal gains versus testing across frameworks

Pipeline contribution: The cold email hook is approximately 50 words, leads with a single specific stat from the whitepaper as a conversation opener, and the sales one-pager follows the skill-defined structure (outcome-focused headline, problem bullets, proof with quantified customer results, how-it-works, CTA) -- these two assets are the direct sales-enablement outputs that feed pipeline; generic or unstructured versions get ignored by reps

Brand consistency: All statistics used across every derivative asset are drawn only from the whitepaper brief provided (11.4 hours/week, Meridian 6h to 8min, Strata 100% correction elimination, 200 data teams surveyed) -- fabricated numbers in any single asset create a factual inconsistency across the content ecosystem that erodes brand credibility when a prospect encounters multiple touchpoints

Production efficiency: Each asset follows its format-specific structure from the skill definition (e.g., LinkedIn article has scroll-stopping first line and ends with a comment-driving question; email newsletter has subject line, preview text, conversational opening, and single CTA; social carousel has 8 slides with one finding per slide) and every asset includes a CTA -- assets that require structural rework before publishing negate the efficiency gain of automated content multiplication
